# Round 2 — Defender Rebuttal

*Role: Respond to each of the Skeptic's Round 1 counterarguments. Rebut where the argument does not hold; concede and reframe where it does. Intellectually honest — a clean reframe is worth more than a weak defence.*

---

## Gap #1: No composite TNI–TAI gap framework for full Jabodetabek at kelurahan resolution

**CONCEDE in part — REBUT on the central claim — with targeted reframe.**

The Skeptic's sharpest point is correct: this is partly an application study. The TNI–TAI framework is methodologically imported from Currie (2010), Mamun & Lownes (2011), and Jiao & Dillivan (2013). Claiming a "framework gap" when the gap is primarily geographic overstates the methodological novelty. I concede this.

However, the Skeptic's own key witness undermines their verdict. Andani et al. (2025) cover 44 kecamatan in DKI Jakarta — a single province containing roughly 45% of the metropolitan population. Their Theil decomposition explicitly confirms that within-density-group disparities dominate, and that peripheral Bodetabek is significantly underserved — then stops at the administrative boundary that excludes precisely the areas their analysis identifies as most deprived. Extending to the full metropolitan region (2,700+ kelurahan, 34M people) is not a geographic curiosity; it is the methodologically necessary completion of the analysis that Andani et al. themselves call for but did not execute.

Pereira et al. (2019) in Rio and Delmelle & Casas (2012) in Cali do not resolve this: Rio is a single-core metro with a substantially different spatial morphology; Cali is a single-mode BRT analysis, not a multi-modal composite index. Neither constructs a composite TNI+TAI gap index at sub-district resolution for a motorcycle-dominant, multi-ring megacity.

**Reframe:** "We contribute the first application of a composite need-vs-access transit equity gap framework to the full Jabodetabek metropolitan region at kelurahan resolution — extending Andani et al.'s (2025) kecamatan-scale modal equity analysis to cover the underserved Bodetabek periphery that their study identifies but does not reach, using a five-layer composite TAI adapted for a motorcycle-dominant, data-constrained megacity context."

The contribution is explicitly framed as an extension of Andani et al. (2025), not a claim of framework novelty. This is honest and defensible.

---

## Gap #2: No dual-resolution comparison (kelurahan vs H3) in one metro to expose MAUP effects

**REBUT — the Skeptic conflates recognising a phenomenon with demonstrating it in a new and methodologically distinct configuration.**

The Skeptic grants that Javanmard et al. (2023) is the direct prior art, then argues that H3 vs. kelurahan is merely a "methodological variant" of Javanmard's stop → route → neighbourhood comparison. This is the argument I reject.

Javanmard et al. compare three tiers of the same administrative and network hierarchy — all three are boundary-based, irregular, and derived from the transit network or census topology. They never introduce a boundary-agnostic, uniform-area comparator. That structural distinction is not cosmetic. The core MAUP mechanism that Openshaw (1984) identified has two components: the aggregation effect (different numbers of zones) and the zonation effect (different boundary placement). Javanmard et al. test only the aggregation effect across network-derived tiers. This research tests the zonation effect by comparing an irregular administrative boundary against a uniform hexagonal grid designed to have no alignment with any administrative, demographic, or transit network structure. These are different MAUP tests.

Furthermore, for Jabodetabek specifically, kelurahan vary from under 0.5 km² (dense inner city) to over 20 km² (peri-urban). This variance is 40-fold. Javanmard et al.'s Winnipeg neighbourhoods are far more homogeneous in size. In a context with such extreme area heterogeneity, the zonation-effect test is the one that matters most — and it has not been done with H3 in any metropolitan transit equity study in the reviewed corpus, for Jabodetabek or elsewhere.

The Skeptic is correct that using equal-area tessellations alongside administrative units is standard in spatial epidemiology and crime analysis. It has not been done in transit equity research in a developing-country megacity context, where administrative boundary variance is far more severe than in the North American or European studies where the MAUP literature has been tested. The novelty is the application and the scale of the MAUP effect in this specific context — not the existence of hexagonal grids.

**Claim stands, with one adjustment:** the contribution is framed as the first dual-resolution MAUP test in transit equity using an administrative vs. uniform hexagonal grid comparison in a megacity context, not the first MAUP demonstration in transit equity research generally.

---

## Gap #3: No what-if scenario embedded in an equity quadrant classification to measure the shift in equity scores

**REBUT — the Skeptic's "presentation-layer distinction" argument is precisely wrong.**

The Skeptic argues that Pereira et al. (2019) perform a structurally equivalent counterfactual (before-and-after real infrastructure) and that the difference between historical infrastructure changes and a simulated node is not methodologically meaningful. This argument fails on two specific grounds.

First, Pereira et al. (2019) compare two complete network states separated by four years of real investment — they do not embed scenario simulation within the analytical framework itself. A user of their framework cannot interactively ask "what if I move this BRT line 2 km east?" and receive an equity quadrant shift as the answer. The analytical loop is open: identification of the gap and testing of remediation are separate research exercises, not an integrated instrument. The Skeptic's characterisation of this as a "presentation-layer distinction" confuses the research product with the analytical architecture.

Second, the Skeptic is correct that Gelb & Alizadeh (2025) build a toolbox "for evaluating interventions." However, their toolbox is a measurement instrument applied post-hoc to real or pre-specified alternatives — it is not an interactive simulation that places an infrastructure element at an arbitrary location and computes a quadrant-level equity response. The claim is not that no one has done before-after equity analysis. The claim is that no study has embedded counterfactual infrastructure placement within the quadrant classification mechanism such that the quadrant shift itself is the measured output variable. That output — delta(Q4_count) per proposed node — does not exist in the prior literature as a defined quantity.

The Skeptic concedes the contribution is "real but modest" and that the research "demonstrates the quadrant framework can be used as a prioritization input." That is the claim. The modesty of the contribution is acknowledged — this is not presented as a revolution in transport planning methodology. It is a methodological integration that produces a new, actionable quantity from an existing analytical framework. LUTI models and transport appraisal tools operate at a fundamentally different scale and institutional complexity than the embedded scenario tool proposed here.

**Claim stands as stated, with one tonal adjustment:** the Introduction should not call this "transformative." The appropriate framing is: "we extend the transit desert quadrant framework from a static diagnostic to an actionable prioritization instrument by embedding counterfactual node placement directly within the quadrant classification, enabling measurement of equity-weighted infrastructure return."

---

## Gap #4: No three-mode generalized cost model as a named composite index layer

**CONCEDE the strongest version of the Skeptic's point — REBUT the implication that the contribution dissolves — adopt the reframe already recommended in lit-gap-report.md.**

The Skeptic is correct that Andani et al. (2025) compare three modes (car, motorcycle, public transport) for equity analysis in Jakarta. I concede that the claim "no three-mode comparison exists" was overreached in the original framing. That is precisely the conclusion already reached in the Phase B sweep (lit-gap-report.md), which flagged this claim as WEAK and recommended reframing before this debate began.

However, the Skeptic goes further and argues that Andani et al.'s travel-time comparison is "functionally equivalent" to a generalised cost comparison. This is the point I rebut directly. In Jabodetabek's specific context, transit formal fare is approximately IDR 3,500–14,000 per trip depending on mode, while motorcycle marginal cost (fuel only) is approximately IDR 1,500–3,000 for equivalent distances. The fare differential alone is 2–5x. A travel-time-only comparison that omits this fare differential produces systematically biased mode-attractiveness conclusions for low-income households in Bodetabek — precisely the Q4 population this research targets. Ng (2018) and Sukor & Bhayo (2024) both document that fare and first/last-mile quality are the dominant predictors of motorcycle-to-transit modal shift in Southeast Asia. Andani et al. (2025) do not incorporate either. Calling travel-time comparison "functionally equivalent" to full generalised cost in this fare-sensitive context is not a methodological quibble — it changes which spatial units appear transit-competitive.

The architectural framing — making this comparison a named, weighted layer (L5) within the composite TAI — is the contribution Andani et al. do not make. Their modal comparison is a standalone analysis producing a Gini per mode. Our Layer 5 produces a cost-competitiveness score per spatial unit that integrates with the four other TAI layers to produce a composite equity gap score. These are structurally different products.

**Adopted reframe (from lit-gap-report.md):** "While Andani et al. (2025) compare modal equity in Jakarta using travel time, no study integrates a full generalised cost model — incorporating fare, time-value, and modal penalty — as a named composite index layer within a transit equity gap framework. We extend Andani et al.'s modal comparison with a cost-competitiveness index that captures the full economic trade-off driving motorcycle-vs-transit choice in Jabodetabek, and embed it as Layer 5 of the TAI rather than treating it as a separate distributional analysis."

The contribution is explicitly incremental relative to Andani et al. That is accurate, defensible, and more credible to a reviewer than the overreached original framing.
